I remember when news was shared through word of mouth, and people had to verify things with care—now, everything seems to appear instantly, without proof. My anxiety, though mild, grows when I see images or claims that seem too good or too dramatic to be true. I have always valued facts and personal experience, especially from my years volunteering in local schools and helping elderly neighbors. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly one hour daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: As a retired man from Lecce who has spent most of his life rooted in community and tradition, I find the rise of fake content on social media deeply troubling. It feels like a distortion of truth, much like a misheard story passed from one generation to the next. I remember when news was shared through word of mouth, and people had to verify things with care—now, everything seems to appear instantly, without proof. My anxiety, though mild, grows when I see images or claims that seem too good or too dramatic to be true. I have always valued facts and personal experience, especially from my years volunteering in local schools and helping elderly neighbors. I don’t trust a post that doesn’t have a clear source or a visible date. I limit my time online to one hour a day, mostly to watch football or catch up on old friends. I now use a simple rule: if it sounds too perfect, too emotional, or too sensational, I question it. I cross-check with trusted local newspapers or family members. I don’t share what I don’t understand. For me, integrity matters more than likes or views. The world is changing fast, but I believe that a quiet, thoughtful approach—like the one I used in my youth—can still protect our shared reality.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a retired, anxious, moderately open man with strong community values and a traditional worldview. His background in local volunteering and personal experience informs a cautious, fact-based approach to media. His high extraversion leads to social engagement, but his anxiety makes him wary of misinformation. The solution must reflect a grounded, personal method rather than technical fixes. The response draws from lived experience, community trust, and personal skepticism, aligning with his values and psychological traits.
